title: Defying gravity: why US Henry Hub natural gas prices are set to rise | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Wood Mackenzie's Horizons report argues US Henry Hub gas will rise to a real US$5/mmbtu by 2035 as power (AI/data-centre), industrial and LNG demand surge while the best acreage depletes, associated gas fades from ~50% to <20% of supply growth, E&P capital discipline holds, and midstream costs climb — with detailed pivot points (integrated LNG producers, traders, PE, frontier/dormant plays, pipeline renaissance, AI demand disappointment) that could temper the forecast.

themes.theme: The core forecast and its stakes

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie forecasts a sustained increase in Henry Hub prices approaching a real US$5/mmbtu by 2035, up from a decade in which average annual prices rarely moved outside a narrow nominal US$2-4/mmbtu range.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: The central call: US gas is entering a structurally higher-price era, a direct input to upstream, LNG and power investment decisions now.
insights.quote: we forecast a sustained increase in Henry Hub prices – approaching a real US$5/mmbtu by 2035
insights.locator: We believe Henry Hub is now entering a new era

insights.claim: Even with the forecast increase Henry Hub prices will remain globally competitive, but a higher price still has significant implications for US industry competitiveness and LNG exports.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: US gas stays cheap relative to the world even at US$5, so the LNG cost advantage narrows but does not disappear.
insights.locator: It is important to note that even with our forecast increase

insights.claim: Higher Henry Hub prices raise domestic producer margins but also push up household power bills with political ramifications, improve renewables' competitiveness in power markets (especially with gas plant construction costs skyrocketing), and lift global LNG prices since US LNG is the marginal-cost fuel.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Second-order effects: long US gas producers, watch renewables gaining relative competitiveness and price-sensitive LNG demand at risk.
insights.locator: Domestic producers will benefit from rising margins

insights.claim: US power sector load is surging on data centres and AI investment, requiring an extra 17 bcfd of gas-fired generation by the mid-2030s, an increase of nearly 50% from 2025 levels.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: AI-driven power load is the dominant demand driver; a modest slowdown would have outsized price implications.
insights.quote: to the tune of an extra 17 bcfd by the mid-2030s, an increase of nearly 50% from 2025 levels
insights.locator: US gas demand growth

insights.claim: US LNG export FIDs hit a record high in 2025 with more reaching FID in 2026, set to more than double US LNG capacity, with aggregate demand expanding into the 2040s and few near-term substitution opportunities to reduce the gas intensity of the US energy system.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Locked-in LNG capacity growth is durable structural demand that tightens the domestic gas balance for two decades.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, final investment decision for new US LNG exports

insights.claim: As the power sector leans increasingly on gas to balance intermittent renewables, demand has become structurally more volatile, with weather a sharp amplifier and underinvested storage limiting the market's ability to absorb swings.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Structurally higher volatility plus thin storage widens the trading range and raises option value on US gas.
insights.locator: Demand variability continues to drive volatility

themes.theme: Supply: resource maturation and E&P discipline

insights.claim: Perpetual high-grading has consumed the best inventory in the Marcellus, Permian and Haynesville year after year, so remaining growth projects will by definition be lower quality, with breakeven costs flattening after years of decline and wells having more complex geology and often being further from market.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Supply cost inflation from reservoir maturation, not falling efficiency, is the structural floor under higher prices.
insights.quote: Supply costs will rise, not because of declining operational efficiency, but because reservoirs have matured. And no new US basins of scale sit untapped.
insights.locator: The best rock is found and producing

insights.claim: Technology gains in mature gas plays now appear incremental, as gains from re-engineered well designs and longer laterals have mostly played out and applications like refracturing never broke out, with material technology investment remaining oil-biased until gas margins compete with liquids plays.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Don't expect a productivity step-change to rescue cheap gas; R&D capital follows oil economics, not gas.
insights.locator: Future technology gains in mature plays appear incremental

insights.claim: Associated gas accounted for about half of US gas supply growth over the last 10 years at low-to-zero marginal cost, but over the next 10 will account for less than 20%, as maturing Permian operators focus on margins over volumes and rig activity slows.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: The fading of free associated gas forces reliance on higher-cost dedicated gas drilling, requiring higher prices to be economic.
insights.quote: Over the last 10 years, associated gas has accounted for about half of US gas supply growth, but over the next 10, it will account for less than 20%.
insights.locator: Decreasing impact of associated gas

insights.claim: Listed US E&Ps remain committed to shareholder returns and capital discipline, even choking back production when cash prices fall below operating costs, so with supply's elasticity to price declining, domestic prices must rise further and persist longer to motivate incremental investment.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Capital discipline means price must do more work to balance the market; the supply response is slower than in the shale-boom era.
insights.locator: Listed US exploration and production (E&P) companies remain committed

themes.theme: Henry Hub as a local, midstream-driven benchmark

insights.claim: Henry Hub remains a localised price shaped by southern Louisiana conditions and is increasingly influenced less by lowest-cost supplies hundreds of miles away and more by the midstream investment required to bring supply to the region, so the forecast sees prices rising with both supply costs and increasing midstream costs.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Rising pipeline/greenfield midstream costs are a distinct upward driver beyond wellhead economics; basis and infrastructure matter.
insights.locator: Henry Hub is the benchmark, but it is still a local price

themes.theme: Pivot points that could temper the outlook

insights.claim: Integrated LNG-upstream operators can invest on long-term contract or international price values rather than local Henry Hub economics, exemplified by EOG's commitment to supply over 700 mmcfd to Cheniere's Corpus Christi Stage III, nearly 60% of it priced on Asian spot, bringing forward South Texas supply that typical listed E&Ps would have deferred.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Vertical LNG-upstream integration is a mechanism for faster supply that could soften prices; a key downside risk to the bull case.
insights.locator: The clearest example today is the growing integration

insights.claim: Non-traditional capital could deliver a faster supply response than the base case: traders like Apex backed by Citadel in the Haynesville can capture value across a trading book, and internationally backed operators including Mitsubishi, JERA, Tokyo Gas and Osaka Gas have built material Haynesville positions and may develop more aggressively than US peers.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Watch M&A migration toward traders/international players and PE as a signal that supply discipline could loosen and cap prices.
insights.locator: Traders are another distinct category

insights.claim: Private equity, which drove earlier aggressive Haynesville development on distinct return profiles, could loosen the supply discipline underpinning the price outlook if quality acreage migrates back toward PE ownership.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Track acreage ownership shifts to PE as a leading indicator of a looser, more price-suppressing supply regime.
insights.locator: Lastly, private equity (PE) cannot be discounted

insights.claim: Dormant pre-shale plays (such as tight gas in the Rockies, driven by data-centre proximity and PE interest) and frontier resources (the western Haynesville/Bossier extension, ultra-deep high-pressure Louisiana reservoirs, and deep gas along the Texas Gulf Coast) could each add a few bcfd, modest but meaningful at the margin.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Frontier/dormant supply is a wildcard that could flatten the price curve if it proves viable at scale; low probability but watch pilots.
insights.locator: The inventory runs deeper still

insights.claim: A modest upward revision to the oil price outlook incentivises more oil-directed drilling and associated gas, but the revised oil price remains well below the levels needed to drive a radical increase in US drilling activity.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Oil price is a swing input to associated-gas supply; the current outlook isn't high enough to flood the gas market.
insights.locator: Oil price and associated gas: an upward revision

themes.theme: Demand-side risks to the bull case

insights.claim: AI/data-centre demand could disappoint via chip-architecture efficiency gains, a cyclical or structural correction in AI investment, or a step-change in battery economics enabling higher renewable penetration, and even a modest slowdown would have price implications given 17 bcfd of incremental power demand is at stake.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: The single biggest risk to the higher-price call is AI power demand underdelivering; monitor chip efficiency and battery economics.
insights.locator: AI/data-centre demand disappoints

insights.claim: As the marginal supplier to the global market, the US would feel any slowdown in demand growth disproportionately, and with US LNG capacity set to exceed one-third of global supply in the early 2030s, buyer concerns about over-concentration could boost non-US projects (Argentina, or even a return of Russian supplies).
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: US LNG scale is a strength that risks becoming a liability if buyers diversify; watch competing supply from Argentina/Russia.
insights.quote: US LNG's scale, currently a commercial strength, risks becoming a liability when buyers begin to treat supplier diversity the way they treat shipping routes or counterparty diversity.
insights.locator: LNG dominance also creates vulnerability

insights.claim: The Middle East conflict is already testing the LNG demand-growth story, with potentially significant implications for feedgas demand and Henry Hub prices.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: Geopolitical shocks can reshape global gas flows and feedgas demand, injecting near-term two-way risk into Henry Hub.
insights.locator: The conflict in the Middle East is already testing

insights.claim: A pipeline renaissance connecting low-cost supply basins to market could compress basis differentials and erode the cost premium of moving molecules, but requires easing local opposition, state regulatory intervention and litigation that have made interstate pipelines slow, costly and high-risk.
insights.speaker: Kristy Kramer
insights.so_what: A credible wildcard: renewed pipeline buildout could put downward pressure on Henry Hub by narrowing basis spreads.
insights.locator: A pipeline renaissance
